entry.title: Running your Github Pages website locally

link.title: Running your Github Pages website locally

entry.content:
My Github contributions were temporarily and artificially boosted when I started my website, see Figure 1. I really did not want my Github contribution overview to be biased because of my inability to build this website. For this particular reason, I decided to figure out how to publish the site locally to build new content and when I am satisfied with the looks and feel, publish it to the online repository.

Figure 1: My Github contributions. The red box indicates contributions from building and troubleshooting my website.

Step 1: install ruby

Since I am currently working from my media server which runs Windows 11, the install steps are (currently?) limited for Windows only. In the future, I might add for Mac and Linux, but installing ruby is normally rather trivial so you could find other sources to help you along.

Firstly, download the RubyInstaller executable from this link. Execute the installer like you would for other Windows programs and make sure to select the option to add the install folder to PATH. If the installer has finished, you should open a terminal (quick access by Windows key + r and enter cmd in the dialog box). In the terminal, enter ruby -v to check if the binary can be found.

Step 2: install ruby dependencies

With ruby installed, the only dependency we need to install is bundle. So run gem install bundle. bundle will require a Gemfile to be present in your repo root. If this is not present create a Gemfile with the following content:

source "https://rubygems.org"

gem "github-pages", group: :jekyll_plugins

If a Gemfile is present, you can build the dependencies using bundle install from the repo root.

Step 3: run the website

After installing the necessary dependencies, the website can be launched via bundle exec jekyll serve. This will by default use localhost:4000 to host the website. In my particular case, port 4000 was not available so I simply routed to port 4001 by executing bundle exec jekyll serve --port 4001. Normally, this will launch your website if the port is available and all you have to do to visit the website is browse to http://localhost:<PORT> to visualize the website, see Figure 2.

Figure 2: Locally hosted version of my website, as indicated by the red box.
entry.summary: My Github contributions were temporarily and artificially boosted when I started my website, see Figure 1. I really did not want my Github contribution overview to be biased because of my inability to build this website. For this particular reason, I decided to figure out how to publish the site locally to build new content and when I am satisfied with the looks and feel, publish it to the online repository.

entry.title: Compiling AMBER for modern GPU architecture

link.title: Compiling AMBER for modern GPU architecture

entry.content:
The dread of every computational chemist is compiling your scientific software. If you rely on campus or government clusters, you can probably rely on real sysadmins. However, when you are running your own system, you have to roll up your sleeves and do the dirty work yourself.

The compilation of the AMBER source code is rather straightforward (see the manual):

  1. make sure to install necessary dependencies
  2. modify the run_cmake to point to the correct install location and include the necessary cmake flags (e.g. MPI, CUDA)
  3. run $ ./run_cmake
  4. run $ make install or $ make install -jX where X is the number of parallel processes and you are done!

For v2 of the cluster, I bought an RTX Pro 4000 card. The RTX Pro 4000 has a brandspanking new Blackwell architecture. Several issues came up after installation of the card:

  1. Black screen on boot. It displayed the startup sequence from motherboard and rendered the BIOS so it had to be a Linux specific issue and not hardware related. I found this post on Reddit which was the exact same issue! The solution? Open source drivers was all it took to correct this error.
  2. When trying the RTX Pro 4000 for AMBER, I encountered an obscure error I had never seen before: cudaMemcpyToSymbol: SetSim copy to cSim failed no kernel image is available for execution on the device. AMBER worked perfectly on the two RTX 4000 ADA cards (older architecture). I did not have to find long for a solution as it appeared quite easily when searching for this particular error in Google. As explained in this link, you need to modify some of the config files. Just for the sake of completeness, I only had 1 of those files in the src present (amber24_src/cmake/CudaConfig.cmake) and the entry on line 17 of that particular file was set(SM120FLAGS -gencode arch=compute_120,code=sm_120) simply changing this line to set(SM120FLAGS -gencode arch=compute_120,code="compute_120,sm_120") was not enough. Lines 19 and 21 had similar content but 100 and 90 instead of 120, respectively. I modified those two lines in the same way and now pmemd.cuda DID startup on the RTX Pro 4000. Great success!

As you can see, doing your own sysadmin is often searching what you do not know and rationalizing whether the info you find makes sense or not. Anyhow, I wanted to quickly jot this down as an example of the ‘‘sysadmin’’ work I do.

See you next time!
entry.summary: The dread of every computational chemist is compiling your scientific software. If you rely on campus or government clusters, you can probably rely on real sysadmins. However, when you are running your own system, you have to roll up your sleeves and do the dirty work yourself.

entry.title: It’s alive!
entry.content:
At the end of August in 2025, v1.0 of my home cluster was fully operational. It was a moment I am still very proud of because I dreamed of having my own little cluster at home ever since venturing in the world of computational chemistry.

My home cluster.

I built this setup by collecting old hardware over the years, oaccompanied by purchases of both new and used parts. One of the used parts is the HP ML350 gen 10 system (middle). Unfortunately, I kept encountering power brake issues when installing GPUs in this machine (something I haven’t been able to fix to date). This issue forced me to setup a Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) VM to provide GPU compute capabilities.

In addition, I had other GPUs available which did not fit in my personal Windows system (since it also houses a RAID card). I bought a used Intel i7 6800K and an X99 motherboard and combined those into a dedicated GPU node. The result, after transferring the hardware into rackmounted cases, is version 1.0 of my own little home compute node!

I am currently working on v2.0. When this update is finished, I will also gives a bit more details about each individual node.

Stay tuned!
entry.summary: At the end of August in 2025, v1.0 of my home cluster was fully operational. It was a moment I am still very proud of because I dreamed of having my own little cluster at home ever since venturing in the world of computational chemistry.
